umbrella-01.text: At what layer does Cisco Umbrella block threats?
options: The DNS layer — before a TCP/UDP connection is ever established
Only at the application layer after connection
umbrella-01.explanation: Umbrella resolves and filters DNS, stopping threats before a connection is even made.

umbrella-01.text: Which IP addresses are Umbrella's primary DNS resolvers?
Does Umbrella require TLS decryption to block encrypted HTTPS C2 traffic?
options: No — it can block the domain at DNS resolution before any HTTPS session forms
Yes, it must decrypt TLS first
It cannot block HTTPS at all
umbrella-01.explanation: Blocking the DNS lookup prevents the HTTPS C2 connection without needing to decrypt anything.

umbrella-01.text: Cisco acquired OpenDNS (now Umbrella) for roughly how much, and when?
umbrella-01.explanation: Cisco bought OpenDNS for ~$635M in 2015, forming the basis of Umbrella.

umbrella-01.text: In the October 2020 Trickbot takedown, which threat actor operated the C2 infrastructure?
umbrella-01.explanation: WIZARD SPIDER ran the Trickbot botnet targeted in the 2020 takedown.

umbrella-01.text: Why is blocking at the DNS layer powerful against malware?
options: Most malware must resolve a domain before connecting, so blocking the lookup cuts off C2 early
umbrella-01.explanation: Since C2 typically starts with a DNS lookup, denying resolution severs the channel pre-connection.

umbrella-01.text: What advantage does DNS-layer enforcement give across a network?
options: It protects any device that uses the resolver, including ones without an agent
It needs an agent on every device
umbrella-01.explanation: Anything resolving through Umbrella is covered, including agentless and IoT/OT devices.

umbrella-01.text: How does DNS-layer security complement endpoint protection?
options: It blocks threats earlier in the chain, reducing what reaches the endpoint
umbrella-01.explanation: Stopping resolution early shrinks the attack surface that endpoint tools must handle.

umbrella-02.text: How does DNS tunneling smuggle data, and why does it bypass strict firewalls?
options: It encodes data in subdomain labels; UDP/53 is rarely blocked
umbrella-02.explanation: Data is hidden in DNS query labels, and since DNS (UDP/53) is almost always allowed, it slips through.

umbrella-02.text: Does a normal user generate about the same DNS query volume as a DNS tunnel?
options: No — a tunnel generates far more queries than a typical user's ~2,000/day
umbrella-02.explanation: Tunnels produce abnormally high query volumes, a key detection signal versus the ~2,000/day baseline.

umbrella-02.text: Which tool fingerprint appeared in the Singapore bank DNS tunnel scenario?
umbrella-02.explanation: The scenario identified dnscat2 v0.07 as the tunneling tool.

umbrella-02.text: Which DNS record type did OilRig (APT34) primarily use for its exfiltration tunnels?
umbrella-02.explanation: OilRig favored TXT records, which can carry larger arbitrary data payloads.

umbrella-02.text: Why is blocking a tunnel domain at the NXDOMAIN level more robust than filtering by subdomain length?
options: It denies the whole domain regardless of how labels are crafted, defeating evasion via label tweaks
umbrella-02.explanation: Domain-level denial isn't fooled by varying label lengths or encodings the way heuristic filters are.

umbrella-02.text: Why are TXT records attractive for DNS exfiltration?
options: They hold relatively large free-form data, maximizing bytes per query
umbrella-02.explanation: TXT records carry arbitrary text, letting attackers pack more data into each response.

umbrella-02.text: What behavioral indicators point to DNS tunneling?
options: High query volume to one domain with long, high-entropy subdomain labels
umbrella-02.explanation: Tunnels show heavy traffic to a single domain with encoded, random-looking subdomains.

umbrella-02.text: How does DNS-layer enforcement counter tunneling exfiltration?
options: By blocking the malicious domain so encoded queries never reach the attacker's nameserver
umbrella-02.explanation: Denying resolution of the tunnel domain cuts the covert channel at its root.

umbrella-03.text: Why are Domain Generation Algorithms (DGAs) effective for resilient C2?
options: Malware generates hundreds of domains daily but the attacker only needs to register one or two
They use a single fixed domain
umbrella-03.explanation: Defenders must block all candidates; the attacker just registers a couple — an asymmetric advantage.

umbrella-03.text: Conficker, the first widespread DGA botnet, generated roughly how many domains per day?
umbrella-03.explanation: Conficker's early DGA produced around 250 candidate domains per day.

umbrella-03.text: Is blocking a single DGA-generated domain enough to stop a DGA botnet?
options: No — the malware rotates through many domains, so one block is quickly bypassed
Only if it's the first domain
umbrella-03.explanation: DGAs cycle through large domain sets, so blocking one leaves many alternatives.

umbrella-03.text: In the NHS hospital scenario, which ransomware was Emotet attempting to deliver?
umbrella-03.explanation: Emotet was staging a Ryuk ransomware delivery in the scenario.

umbrella-03.text: What does Umbrella's ML-based DGA detection flag?
options: NXDOMAIN storms combined with high-entropy, machine-generated-looking domain names
Only known-bad domains from a static list
Low query volumes to popular sites
umbrella-03.explanation: Bursts of failed lookups plus algorithmic, high-entropy names are the DGA signature.

options: Most generated domains aren't registered, so lookups return NXDOMAIN until the live one is found
umbrella-03.explanation: Trying many unregistered candidates yields many NXDOMAIN responses — a detectable pattern.

umbrella-03.text: Why is algorithmic/ML detection better than blocklists for DGAs?
options: It catches never-before-seen generated domains that static lists miss
umbrella-03.explanation: DGA domains are novel daily, so behavioral/ML detection beats reactive blocklists.

umbrella-03.text: What is the core defender challenge posed by DGAs?
options: They must block or predict many domains while the attacker registers just one
They only face one domain ever
umbrella-03.explanation: The volume asymmetry favors attackers, which is why predictive/ML detection is essential.

umbrella-04.text: In single-flux fast flux, what rotates?
options: The IP addresses behind one domain rotate rapidly, while the domain name stays the same
The domain name itself rotates every few minutes
umbrella-04.explanation: Single-flux keeps the domain fixed but rapidly cycles the A-record IPs.

umbrella-04.text: Double-flux extends single-flux by also rotating which record type?
umbrella-04.explanation: Double-flux rotates the authoritative nameservers (NS) too, adding resilience.

umbrella-04.text: Are IP-based blocklists an effective long-term defense against fast flux?
options: No — the IPs rotate constantly, so blocklists go stale almost immediately
umbrella-04.explanation: Rapid IP rotation defeats IP blocklists, which can't keep pace.

umbrella-04.text: How does Umbrella defeat fast flux?
options: By blocking at the domain-name level, making IP rotation irrelevant
umbrella-04.explanation: Blocking the domain regardless of which IP it points to neutralizes the IP churn.

umbrella-04.text: In the trading firm scenario, how many unique IPs did flux-c2.net resolve to over 24 hours?
umbrella-04.explanation: The domain cycled through 287 distinct IPs in a day — a clear fast-flux signal.

umbrella-04.text: What DNS pattern indicates a fast-flux network?
options: A single domain resolving to many short-lived IPs with very low TTLs
One stable IP with a long TTL
umbrella-04.explanation: Many rotating IPs and very low TTLs on one domain are the hallmark of fast flux.

umbrella-04.text: Why do attackers use fast flux for C2 infrastructure?
options: To keep C2 reachable and resilient against takedowns and IP blocking
umbrella-04.explanation: Constant rotation makes the infrastructure hard to take down or block by IP.

umbrella-04.text: Why is the domain name the right enforcement point for flux defense?
options: It is the stable element attackers can't rotate without losing victims' lookups
umbrella-04.explanation: The domain must stay reachable for victims, so blocking it is durable against IP/NS flux.

umbrella-05.text: How does a DNS rebinding attack work?
options: A domain's DNS record changes to a private IP after the TTL expires, so the browser treats an internal resource as same-origin
umbrella-05.explanation: Rebinding swaps the resolved IP to an internal RFC-1918 address, abusing the same-origin policy.

umbrella-05.text: How does Umbrella detect DNS rebinding?
options: By flagging public domains that return RFC-1918 private IP addresses in their responses
umbrella-05.explanation: A public domain resolving to a private IP is the tell-tale rebinding indicator.

umbrella-05.text: After a successful rebind, does the browser block the internal request as cross-origin?
options: No — because the origin (domain) is unchanged, the browser treats it as same-origin and allows it
umbrella-05.explanation: The domain stays the same, so same-origin policy permits the request to the now-internal IP.

umbrella-05.text: What must an attacker control to perform DNS rebinding?
options: The authoritative nameserver for the malicious domain
umbrella-05.explanation: Controlling the authoritative nameserver lets the attacker change the resolved IP at will.

umbrella-05.text: In the IoT lab scenario, what device was exposed at 192.168.10.1?
umbrella-05.explanation: The internal target was the TP-Link AX3000 lab router at 192.168.10.1.

umbrella-05.text: Which browser security mechanism does DNS rebinding subvert?
umbrella-05.explanation: Rebinding tricks the same-origin policy into allowing access to internal resources.

umbrella-05.text: Why is flagging public domains that return private IPs an effective defense?
options: Legitimate public domains shouldn't resolve to RFC-1918 space, so it's a reliable rebinding signal
umbrella-05.explanation: Public-to-private resolution is abnormal and strongly indicates a rebinding attempt.

umbrella-05.text: Why is a short TTL central to DNS rebinding?
options: It lets the attacker quickly switch the record from their server to the internal IP
umbrella-05.explanation: A low TTL forces a fast re-resolution, enabling the swap to the private IP.

umbrella-06.text: How do IDN homograph attacks deceive users?
options: They use Unicode characters that look identical to ASCII letters, hiding the malicious domain in plain sight
umbrella-06.explanation: Visually identical Unicode glyphs make a lookalike domain indistinguishable to the eye in older browsers.

umbrella-06.text: Is a Let's Encrypt TLS certificate on a lookalike domain a reliable sign the site is legitimate?
options: No — anyone can obtain a free cert; it proves encryption, not legitimacy
umbrella-06.explanation: Free certificates secure the connection but say nothing about whether the domain is trustworthy.

umbrella-06.text: Can Umbrella's lookalike detection catch one-character typosquats?
options: Yes — it identifies near-match and typosquat domains, not just exact matches
umbrella-06.explanation: Lookalike detection flags typosquats and visually similar domains, not just exact strings.

umbrella-06.text: In the Pentagon contractor scenario, what substitution made the lookalike hard to spot?
options: A zero ('0') replacing the letter 'a' at position 8
A capital I for lowercase l
umbrella-06.explanation: Swapping '0' for 'a' produced a near-invisible lookalike domain.

umbrella-06.text: NOBELIUM (APT29) is attributed to which nation-state service?
umbrella-06.explanation: NOBELIUM / APT29 is attributed to Russia's SVR foreign intelligence service.

umbrella-06.text: How are IDN homograph domains represented at the DNS level?
options: As Punycode (xn--) encodings of the Unicode characters
umbrella-06.explanation: Internationalized domains are encoded in Punycode (xn--...), which detection can inspect.

umbrella-06.text: Why can't users reliably defend against homographs by 'looking carefully'?
options: The malicious characters are visually identical, so the eye can't distinguish them
umbrella-06.explanation: Because the glyphs are identical, automated detection is needed — visual inspection fails.

umbrella-06.text: What is the most reliable defense against lookalike/homograph phishing domains?
options: Automated lookalike detection at the DNS layer plus user awareness
umbrella-06.explanation: DNS-layer lookalike detection catches what users and TLS cannot.

umbrella-07.text: In Umbrella's policy hierarchy, does a custom allow-list entry override security category blocks like Malware and C2?
options: Yes — allow-list entries take precedence, which is why they're dangerous if misused
umbrella-07.explanation: Allow-list entries override security categories, so a bad entry can punch a hole through protection.

umbrella-07.text: Which allow-list approach follows Umbrella best practice?
options: A specific domain like pkg.update-service.io with an expiry, scoped to the WORKSTATIONS group
A broad wildcard *.io with no expiry
umbrella-07.explanation: Narrow, scoped, time-bound entries minimize the risk an allow-list creates.

umbrella-07.text: In the school district scenario, was the rogue allow-list rule (RULE-2023-0092) added by an account still active at the time of the incident?
options: No — it was added by an account that was no longer active, a key red flag
umbrella-07.explanation: The rule traced to a since-deactivated account — an indicator of compromise/insider abuse.

umbrella-07.text: How did LockBit operators exploit the wildcard allow-list entry?
options: By registering a subdomain under the permitted wildcard domain
umbrella-07.explanation: A wildcard allow let them stand up an allowed subdomain for C2, bypassing blocks.

umbrella-07.text: How much did the school district LockBit attack ultimately cost?
umbrella-07.explanation: The incident totaled roughly $2.1M in ransom and recovery costs.

umbrella-07.text: Why are wildcard allow-list entries especially risky?
options: They permit every subdomain, which attackers can freely register under
umbrella-07.explanation: A wildcard trusts an entire domain space, giving attackers room to create allowed C2 hosts.

options: Scope narrowly, set expiry dates, review regularly, and audit who adds entries
umbrella-07.explanation: Narrow scope, expiry, periodic review, and change auditing keep allow-lists from becoming back doors.

umbrella-07.text: What audit finding should trigger investigation of an allow-list rule?
options: A rule added by a deactivated or unexpected account
umbrella-07.explanation: Entries from inactive/unknown accounts are strong indicators of malicious modification.

umbrella-08.text: If malware uses DoH (DNS over HTTPS) with a hardcoded resolver like 1.1.1.1:443, does Umbrella's standard DNS resolver see and block those queries?
options: No — the queries bypass the configured resolver by going encrypted to an external DoH endpoint
Only if TLS is decrypted at the firewall automatically
DoH cannot be used by malware
umbrella-08.explanation: Hardcoded external DoH bypasses the standard resolver, so DNS-layer visibility is lost unless DoH is controlled.

umbrella-08.text: Godlua (2019), the first malware confirmed to use DoH for C2 evasion, targeted what?
options: Linux and Windows servers across Asia-Pacific
umbrella-08.explanation: Godlua pioneered DoH-based C2 against Linux/Windows servers in Asia-Pacific.

umbrella-08.text: Does Umbrella's roaming client block all DoH connections, including to Umbrella's own cloud DoH endpoint?
options: No — it permits Umbrella's own DoH endpoint while controlling rogue external DoH
Yes, it blocks every DoH connection
umbrella-08.explanation: The roaming client allows sanctioned Umbrella DoH while steering/blocking unauthorized DoH.

umbrella-08.text: In the Tokyo scenario, was svchost32.exe a legitimate Windows process?
options: No — svchost32.exe is a masquerade; the real process is svchost.exe
Yes, it's a core system process
umbrella-08.explanation: svchost32.exe mimics the legitimate svchost.exe to hide malicious DoH activity.

umbrella-08.text: The decoded DoH payload in the Tokyo scenario revealed a query for which domain?
umbrella-08.explanation: Decoding the DoH traffic exposed a lookup for the C2 domain c2-prime.net.

umbrella-08.text: Why does DoH complicate DNS-layer security?
options: It encrypts DNS inside HTTPS, hiding queries from network DNS inspection
It is slower than normal DNS
umbrella-08.explanation: Wrapping DNS in HTTPS conceals the queries from traditional DNS-layer visibility.

umbrella-08.text: How can an organization regain DNS visibility against rogue DoH?
options: Block unauthorized external DoH resolvers and funnel DNS through sanctioned (Umbrella) resolvers
umbrella-08.explanation: Blocking rogue DoH endpoints and enforcing approved resolvers restores inspection.

umbrella-08.text: What is a useful detection for DoH-based C2 like the Tokyo case?
options: Spotting masquerading process names and unexpected outbound HTTPS to known DoH endpoints
umbrella-08.explanation: Masquerade names plus anomalous DoH-bound HTTPS are strong indicators worth investigating.

umbrella-09.text: What does passive DNS enable analysts to do?
options: Review historical DNS resolutions to find domains that shared IPs with known C2 servers
umbrella-09.explanation: Passive DNS history lets analysts pivot from a known-bad IP to other domains that resolved to it.

umbrella-09.text: How quickly does Talos push confirmed malicious domains to Umbrella globally?
options: Faster than ~4 hours — propagation is rapid, not a multi-hour delay
umbrella-09.explanation: Talos intelligence propagates to Umbrella quickly, well under a slow multi-hour window.

umbrella-09.text: In the Scattered Spider (UNC3944) campaign, did Talos block infrastructure only after most attacks succeeded?
options: No — blocking occurred before the majority of attacks succeeded
umbrella-09.explanation: Talos disrupted the campaign infrastructure ahead of most attack success.

umbrella-09.text: How many domains were identified in the Scattered Spider 2023 campaign via passive DNS pivoting?
umbrella-09.explanation: Pivoting through passive DNS surfaced 47 related campaign domains.

umbrella-09.text: What did the 47 Scattered Spider domains have in common?
options: Registered at the same registrar within a few days, paid with cryptocurrency
Registered years apart on different registrars
umbrella-09.explanation: Shared registrar, tight timeframe, and crypto payment tied the infrastructure together.

umbrella-09.text: Why is IP-based pivoting through passive DNS powerful for threat hunting?
options: One known-bad indicator can reveal an entire related infrastructure cluster
umbrella-09.explanation: Shared hosting and registration patterns let a single IP expose a whole campaign.

umbrella-09.text: How does passive DNS + threat intel enable proactive defense?
options: Related malicious domains can be blocked before victims ever query them
It only works after a breach
umbrella-09.explanation: Identifying campaign infrastructure early lets defenders block it ahead of use.

umbrella-09.text: Which registration traits help cluster attacker infrastructure?
options: Same registrar, near-simultaneous registration, and anonymous (crypto) payment
umbrella-09.explanation: Common registrar, tight timing, and crypto payment are classic infrastructure fingerprints.

umbrella-10.text: Can DNS logs identify compromised hosts — including unmanaged OT/SCADA — faster than EDR telemetry alone?
options: Yes — DNS sees any device that resolves, including those without an EDR agent
umbrella-10.explanation: Because OT/SCADA often can't run EDR, DNS visibility uniquely catches their C2 lookups.

umbrella-10.text: Did VOLT TYPHOON primarily rely on custom malware with a heavy exploit footprint?
options: No — it favored living-off-the-land techniques with minimal malware footprint
umbrella-10.explanation: Volt Typhoon is known for stealthy living-off-the-land activity, not noisy custom malware.

umbrella-10.text: What does DNS containment achieve against C2?
options: It blocks the C2 domain for all infected hosts at once, including unmanaged OT/SCADA
It only isolates one managed host
It requires an agent per device
umbrella-10.explanation: Blocking the domain centrally severs C2 for every device that resolves through Umbrella simultaneously.

umbrella-10.text: In the ERCOT scenario, how many OT hosts were found querying the VOLT TYPHOON C2 domain?
umbrella-10.explanation: DNS logs revealed 7 OT hosts reaching out to the C2 domain.

umbrella-10.text: What do CISA's Critical Infrastructure Protection Guidelines (CPG 2.S) recommend regarding DNS?
options: DNS-layer security as a baseline control for critical infrastructure operators
umbrella-10.explanation: CPG 2.S endorses DNS-layer security as a baseline protective control for critical infrastructure.

umbrella-10.text: Why is DNS-layer monitoring especially valuable in OT/SCADA environments?
options: Those devices often can't run endpoint agents, so DNS is one of the few telemetry sources
umbrella-10.explanation: Agentless OT/SCADA leaves DNS as a critical, often sole, source of compromise visibility.

umbrella-10.text: Why does DNS telemetry help detect living-off-the-land actors like Volt Typhoon?
options: Even stealthy actors must resolve C2 domains, leaving a DNS trail
LOTL actors never use the network
umbrella-10.explanation: Minimal-malware actors still need C2 resolution, which DNS logging captures.

umbrella-10.text: What is the advantage of DNS-layer containment during an active intrusion?
options: It instantly cuts C2 for all affected hosts network-wide, including unmanaged devices
It has no effect on OT
umbrella-10.explanation: A single domain block simultaneously severs C2 across every resolving device.
